message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-009: run_study never persists or correlates the provenance trail to its MLflow run (success and failure paths)
File: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:351
Description: run_study records a hash-chained provenance trail and stores experiment_run_id inside the run_end entry (pipeline.py:721), but never writes the trail anywhere durable nor attaches it to the experiment run via tracker.log_artifact, even when a tracker and run_id are known. Persistence is opt-in (StudyResult.persist_trail() at :226 + a manual tracker.log_artifact). So the default governed run produces an MLflow run with metrics but no link back to the append-only trail, and the trail evaporates on process exit. The failure path is worse: the run_failed entry (pipeline.py:732) omits experiment_run_id even though run_id is in scope and the finally block terminates the MLflow run as FAILED (:754) — the case where correlation matters most leaves a FAILED run and an in-memory-only trail sharing no recorded id. GOVERNANCE §3 calls the trail 'the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim'.
PoC: run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()): on success the MLflow run has params/metrics but zero artifacts and no provenance.json; StudyResult.provenance is gone on exit. With a discover() that raises, the run is set FAILED but run_failed has no experiment_run_id — given the FAILED run id you cannot find its trail, and vice versa.
Suggested fix: When a tracker and run_id are present, have run_study persist the trail to a working path and tracker.log_artifact(run_id, path) on the success path (best-effort partial trail on failure), guarded like the other track() calls. Add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed trail.record() payload (deterministic, preserves hash-determinism) so failed runs are equally correlatable across the trail and the experiment store.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

702	        if verify_trail:
703	            # Re-verify the whole chain before handing back the result. This re-hashes every
704	            # entry — O(N) — which is redundant for an in-memory trail this call built and never
705	            # mutated (each entry was hashed correctly when appended). It is a cheap safety net in
706	            # the common case but a real cost for very large trails, so a perf-sensitive caller
707	            # that trusts the in-process trail may pass verify_trail=False (pass 3, #F-036). The
708	            # important re-verification — of a trail deserialized from an external store — is the
709	            # caller's verify_records() on from_records(), which this flag does not affect.
710	            trail.verify()  # the provenance chain must be intact before we hand back the result
711	        # Terminal success entry (pass 3, #F-006): a phase 'report' record is not a run-level
712	        # terminus, so a trail truncated mid-run is otherwise indistinguishable from one that
713	        # completed. Record run_end ONLY after verify() passes, so its presence as the last
714	        # entry is an affirmative "this run finished with an intact chain" marker. The payload is
715	        # deterministic (no wall-clock) so the provenance-hash determinism guarantee holds — the
716	        # non-deterministic run duration goes to the experiment tracker as an SLO metric instead.
717	        trail.record(
718	            "run_end",
719	            f"study {defn.name!r} completed successfully",
720	            study=defn.name,
721	            experiment_run_id=run_id,
722	            n_provenance_entries=len(trail.entries) + 1,  # +1 counts this terminal entry itself
723	        )
724	        track(_count_logger("run_duration_s", time.monotonic() - started_at))  # SLO metric (#F-029)
725	        ok = True
726	    except Exception as exc:
727	        # Record a terminal failure entry BEFORE re-raising so the abort reason is recoverable
728	        # from the (caller-owned) trail, not lost (#109).
729	        last_phase = visited[-1].name if visited else "start"
730	        try:
731	            trail.record(
732	                "run_failed",
733	                f"{type(exc).__name__} during {last_phase}",
734	                error_type=type(exc).__name__,
735	                message=str(exc)[:2000],
736	                last_phase=last_phase,
737	            )
738	        except Exception as inner:  # noqa: BLE001 — never mask the original failure
739	            # The run_failed entry could not be recorded (payload unserializable, clock raised,
740	            # trail lock contended). Don't silently swallow — emit a structured warning so the
741	            # failure reason is at least in the logs, even though it is absent from the trail
742	            # (pass 3, #F-030). The original exception is still re-raised below.
743	            _log.warning(

747	            )

755	            except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001,S110 — best-effort cleanup
756	                pass

The finding is confirmed in the code. Let me verify the two claims precisely:

1. Success path: `run_end` records `experiment_run_id=run_id` (line 721). Let me check if anywhere there's a `log_artifact` call for the trail.
2. Failure path: `run_failed` (lines 731-737) omits `experiment_run_id`.

content.text:
Verification complete. All claims in the finding are accurate against the current code.

**Findings:**

1. **No trail persistence / log_artifact in run_study** — Confirmed. `log_artifact` exists on the tracker (`tracking.py:92`), but `grep` shows zero calls in `pipeline.py`. The only persistence mechanism is `StudyResult.persist_trail()` (pipeline.py:226), which is opt-in and caller-driven, with its docstring (line 232) explicitly telling the *caller* to call `tracker.log_artifact` afterward. So a default governed run via `run_study(..., tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker())` produces an MLflow run with params/metrics but no provenance artifact, and `StudyResult.provenance` is in-memory-only — it evaporates on process exit. Confirmed.

2. **Success path correlation** — `run_end` (line 717-723) does record `experiment_run_id=run_id`, so the trail-side carries the id. But the experiment-store side has no link back (no artifact), so correlation is one-directional at best on success.

3. **Failure path omits experiment_run_id** — Confirmed. The `run_failed` record (lines 731-737) payload is `error_type`, `message`, `last_phase` only — no `experiment_run_id`, even though `run_id` is in scope. Meanwhile the `finally` block (lines 749-756) terminates the MLflow run as `FAILED`. So a FAILED MLflow run and its in-memory trail share no recorded id in either direction. The PoC (`discover()` raises → FAILED run + uncorrelatable trail) is reproducible.

**Severity assessment:** This is a genuine production-readiness/governance concern, not speculative. GOVERNANCE §3 calls the trail "the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim." The default governed configuration (tracker present) produces non-durable, non-correlated provenance — exactly the case the project's independence/reproducibility claims depend on. However, it is not a crash/data-corruption/security issue and a `persist_trail()` workaround exists for callers; the trail is also returned in the `StudyResult` on success. The most acute gap (failure path with no correlation, no artifact) leaves an auditor unable to tie a FAILED run to its abort reason. I rate this **medium**: real correctness-of-governance defect with a fully reproducible PoC, but bounded by an existing opt-in API and by being limited to the optional-tracker path rather than the core data path.

input.reasoning: Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py. (1) run_study never calls tracker.log_artifact: grep shows zero log_artifact references in pipeline.py despite the method existing on the tracker (tracking.py:92). The only trail persistence is StudyResult.persist_trail() (pipeline.py:226), which is opt-in/caller-driven; its docstring (line 232) explicitly instructs the CALLER to call tracker.log_artifact afterward. So a default run_study(..., tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) yields an MLflow run with params/metrics but no provenance artifact, and StudyResult.provenance is in-memory only — gone on process exit. PoC reproducible. (2) Success path: run_end (pipeline.py:717-723) does record experiment_run_id=run_id, but the experiment store has no reciprocal link (no artifact), so correlation is one-directional. (3) Failure path: run_failed (pipeline.py:731-737) payload is only error_type/message/last_phase — experiment_run_id is omitted even though run_id is in scope; meanwhile the finally block (lines 751-754) terminates the MLflow run as FAILED. So a FAILED run and its trail share no recorded id in either direction — the discover()-raises PoC is reproducible. The fix (best-effort log_artifact on success guarded like other track() calls + adding experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed record, which is deterministic and preserves hash-determinism) is correct and necessary. This is a genuine governance/reproducibility defect (GOVERNANCE §3 names the trail the spine of reproducibility), not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design.

content.text: Confirmed G-009 as a true medium-severity finding. Details are in the StructuredOutput call above; the load-bearing evidence is at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:226 (opt-in persist_trail), :721 (success records experiment_run_id), :731-737 (run_failed omits experiment_run_id), :751-754 (FAILED termination), and the absence of any log_artifact call in pipeline.py despite tracking.py:92 providing one.
